confidence.disclosure: Al-Battani's astronomical observations are precisely dated (he records them by year in his Sabi'an Zij), but his personal biography is sparse. We know he was born into a Sabian family near Harran in northern Mesopotamia, that he conducted observations from Raqqa for forty years, and that he died on the road back from Baghdad in 929. Specific dates outside of the observation log are inferences.

sources: C.A. Nallino, Al-Battani sive Albatenii Opus Astronomicum (1899–1907)

journey.event: Born around this time near Harran in northern Mesopotamia, into a family of the Sabians of Harran — a community that had preserved late-antique Greek learning and Babylonian star-religion for centuries. His father Jabir ibn Sinan is a maker of astronomical instruments.

Learns astronomy and mathematics from his father, and inherits the instrument-making craft and the Sabian astronomical tradition. Unlike most other Sabians, he converts to Islam at some point in his life.

Moves to Raqqa on the upper Euphrates, then a flourishing Abbasid city that had served as Harun al-Rashid's residence. Al-Battani begins the systematic programme of observations that will run for forty years.

Records his earliest dated solar and stellar observations from Raqqa. Over the following decades he will determine the length of the solar year to within minutes, the obliquity of the ecliptic, and the motion of the sun's apogee — discoveries cited centuries later by Tycho Brahe and Kepler.

Conducts observations from Antakya (Antioch) of the equinoxes, comparing his results with Ptolemy and finding the older Greek values inaccurate. He correctly attributes the difference to the slow precession of the equinoxes.

Compiles his observations and calculations into the Kitab al-Zij al-Sabi, the Sabi'an Astronomical Tables. The work will be translated into Latin in the 12th century as De Motu Stellarum and circulate in Europe for five hundred years.
Continues observations into old age. He introduces several Indian trigonometric methods into Arabic astronomy, replacing Greek chord tables with sines, and computes the first table of cotangents.

Travels to Baghdad, with a delegation from the people of Raqqa, to lodge a complaint with the caliph against unjust taxation. On the return journey he dies near Samarra, in the village of Qasr al-Jiss. His tomb is reportedly there but has not survived.
